message: The ANTISCAM dataset contains 220 human-human dialogs. It also includes 3,044 annotated sentences in 100 dialogs.

Intent is annotated with a hierarchical scheme that first separates on-task and off-task intents, where on-task intents are task-specific key actions and off-task intents are annotated using common dialog acts. Conversations are segmented into single sentences, and each sentence is annotated rather than whole turns.
TransferTransfo and the hybrid model.

The model performance is evaluated with automatic metrics and human evaluation metrics. Automatic metrics are Perplexity (PPL), Response-Intent Prediction (RIP), Response-Slot Prediction (RSP), Extended Response-Intent Prediction (ERIP), and Extended Response-Slot Prediction (ERSP); RIP/RSP compare predicted response intents and slots against human-annotated ground truth, while ERIP/ERSP allow partial credit based on acceptable intent transitions estimated from training-data transition probabilities. Human evaluation uses Fluency, Coherence, Engagement, dialog Length, and Task Success Score (TaskSuc), where TaskSuc is the average type of information obtained from attackers such as name, address, and phone number.

OpenTapioca does not report accuracy, only F1 scores. Compared to the best system shown, it is state-of-the-art only on ISTEX-1000 with 0.870 micro / 0.858 macro F1 versus DBP Spotlight’s 0.574 / 0.575, but it is below the best on AIDA-CoNLL (0.482 / 0.399 vs 0.725 / 0.684), Microposts 2016 (0.087 / 0.515 vs 0.056 / 0.790 or 0.729 micro), and RSS-500 (0.335 / 0.310 vs 0.455 / 0.447).

They compare against two state-of-the-art early rumour detection baselines: Liu et al. (2015) and Yang et al. (2012). More generally, the related work includes methods based on clustering, sentiment analysis, and propagation graphs, including Cai et al. (2014), Zhao et al. (2015), Qazvinian et al. (2011), Wu et al. (2015), Kwon et al. (2013), Wang et al. (2015), and Zhou et al. (2015).

They report accuracy to evaluate effectiveness, and additionally use the standard TDT evaluation procedure with Detection Error Trade-off (DET) curves showing the trade-off between miss and false alarm probability. They also evaluate efficiency by throughput per second.

No. Their approach uses an SVM classifier trained on labeled rumours and non-rumours, but it also uses pseudo feedback, which treats previously high-scoring documents as “pseudo” rumours during detection rather than relying only on true labels.

Yes. They state that because no official datasets were available, they created their own dataset, including 202 confirmed rumours from Sina Weibo’s official rumour debunking service and 202 non-rumours.
Chinese.
They define a rumour as information of doubtful or unconfirmed truth. They also treat unconfirmed (novel) information with respect to trusted or official news sources as an indication of rumours.
They compared with LDA, Doc-NADE, HTMM, and GMNTM.
The paper explores generative document evaluation, document classification, and the topic2sentence task of generating sentences given a topic.
They use six NER datasets: CoNLL2003 and OntoNotes 5.0 for English, and Chinese OntoNotes 4.0, MSRA, Weibo, and Resume for Chinese.

They replace absolute positional encoding with relative positional encoding \(R_{t-j}\), where the attention score is \(A^{rel}_{t,j}=Q_tK_j^T+Q_tR_{t-j}^T+uK_j^T+vR_{t-j}^T\). Because \(R_t\) and \(R_{-t}\) have identical cosine terms but opposite sine terms, the model can distinguish forward vs. backward positions while also encoding relative distance.

Yes. The paper states that TENER achieves state-of-the-art performance among models without pre-trained language models or designed features, and under the same pre-trained embeddings and external knowledge, it outperforms previous models on all six datasets.
KALM achieved 95.6% accuracy. KALM-QA achieved 95% accuracy for parsing general queries and 100% accuracy on the MetaQA dataset.

The state-of-the-art systems mentioned are SEMAFOR, SLING, and the Stanford KBP system for knowledge extraction, and the state-of-the-art machine learning approach on MetaQA is Variational Reasoning Network.

They evaluated KALM-QA on two datasets: manually constructed general questions based on the 50 logical frames, and the MetaQA dataset. For KALM, they evaluated on 250 manually constructed sentences adapted from FrameNet exemplar sentences.

The paper’s contributions are: (i) adapting a state-of-the-art seq2seq neural system to Italian event detection and classification in a single-step approach, (ii) investigating the quality of existing Italian word embeddings for this task, and (iii) comparing against a state-of-the-art discrete classifier. It also makes the pre-trained models and scripts publicly available.

The paper compares against the best EVENTI Subtask B system, FBK-HLT, a cascade of two SVM classifiers (one for detection and one for classification) based on rich linguistic features. It also uses five embedding-based Bi-LSTM-CRF configurations as comparison settings: Berardi2015 w2v, Berardi2015 GloVe, Fastext-It, ILC-ItWack, and DH-FBK 100.
Unanswerable

They decide a cluster’s semantic concept label in three steps: first, they assign each cluster word to a WordNet synset by choosing the set of synsets that minimizes total distance among them; second, they generate candidate labels as the union of all hypernyms of those chosen synsets; third, they rank candidate labels by the sum of their WordNet pathwise distances to the chosen synsets, preferring the labels with the smallest total distance. The top-ranked candidate is the semantic concept label.

They discover coherent word clusters by first training domain-specific Word2Vec CBOW embeddings, then applying k-means to the embeddings of the gender-associated words. Because k-means can get stuck in local optima, they run it 50 times and keep the clustering with the lowest sum of squared errors.
Each of the two introduced datasets consists of over 300K sentences. More specifically, the celebrity dataset has 342,645 sentences and the professor dataset has 976,677 sentences.

The strong baselines were the random baseline of 20% for the Word Intrusion task and the cluster centroid used as a baseline label for cluster labeling. The centroid baseline achieved an in-cluster rate of .60 and an out-of-cluster rate of .18.

Data-driven systems rank low in general. The clean Seq2Seq often produces flirtatious responses, while the IR approach produces mostly retaliatory responses followed by flirtatious ones; only Neuralconvo and Cleverbot ever politely refuse.
They collected 9960 HITs from 472 crowd workers. After filtering, 18,826 scores remained, averaging 7.7 ratings per individual system reply.
14 response types were evaluated. Categories (1a) and (1b) were excluded from the human study.
Yes. The automatically annotated time expressions were evaluated: the customised HeidelTime version achieved an F1 value of 89.1 for partial identification and normalization.
Agglutinative and fusional morphological typologies are considered.

Yes. The paper explicitly contrasts derivational and inflectional morphemes, noting that inflectional morphemes give information on word-level semantics, while derivational morphemes provide more clues on sentence-level semantics.

Gold morphological features are used as outputs of morph-language. For all languages, these consist of the lemma followed by language-specific morphological tags, with some exceptions such as additional POS tags for Turkish.

The paper uses Semantic Scholar (SemScholar), Springer Nature’s SciGraph, the Textbook Question Answering (TQA) corpus, the January 2018 English Wikipedia dataset, and the Flickr30K and COCO datasets.
English.

The paper explores two ad-hoc approaches as baselines: a direct combination baseline, which computes figure-caption correspondence through the scalar product between the softmax outputs of separately trained figure and caption classifiers, and a supervised pre-training baseline, which freezes the feature extraction trunks of those classifiers and then trains the FCC task on top of them. It also refers to prior domain-specific ad-hoc methods such as training specific classifiers for each diagram component and connector type plus another model to ground the semantics of the diagram parse graph.
They compared against two supervised baselines: a direct combination baseline and a supervised pre-training baseline. Both were trained on a classification task against the SciGraph taxonomy.
Yes. The paper states that the scientific corpora are specific to the scientific domain, and that domain relevance is more significant than sheer volume.

They come from scientific publications: the paper uses a corpus of scientific figures and captions extracted from SN SciGraph and AI2 Semantic Scholar. For Semantic Scholar, figures and captions were extracted from downloaded PDFs using PDFFigures2.

No. The paper states that while lexicons could potentially help and top-performing approaches may rely on external information, this system “do[es] not experiment adding any additional sources of information as input,” including lexicons.
No. They explicitly state that they “do not experiment adding any additional sources of information as input,” such as lexicons.
The baseline was the Weka baseline.

Their model outperformed the Weka baseline on the development set but performed worse on the test set, and in the shared task it placed 13th out of 22 competitors. The best average Pearson correlations were 0.689 on development and 0.522 on test, compared to the baseline’s 0.611 and 0.648.
The training, validation, and test datasets provided for the WASSA-2017 Shared Task on Emotion Intensity were used, consisting of tweets for four emotions: joy, sadness, fear, and anger.

The paper does not give a single formal definition of hate speech, but characterizes it as speech that “promotes the dehumanization of people who are typically from minority groups” and can incite hate crime. In annotation, they treat it as comments that may be direct or indirect and discriminate against individuals or groups through hostility types such as abusive, hateful, offensive, disrespectful, or fearful out of ignorance.
English, French, and Arabic.

The paper considers five aspects: (a) whether the text is direct or indirect; (b) whether it is offensive, disrespectful, hateful, fearful out of ignorance, abusive, or normal; (c) the attribute on which it discriminates against an individual or group; (d) the name of that group; and (e) how annotators feel about its content on a range from negative to neutral sentiments.

Their final dataset contains 13,014 tweets: 5,647 English, 4,014 French, and 3,353 Arabic, plus a pilot corpus of 100 tweets per language. The paper also describes it as around 13,000 potentially derogatory tweets.

Semantic Web technologies offer opportunities to improve MT quality by making semantics explicit and using disambiguated knowledge about entities, properties, and relationships from Linked Data/knowledge graphs to resolve lexical, semantic, and syntactic ambiguity, including named entities and OOV words. They can also support translation of non-standard speech through user/context modeling and enable the translation and enrichment of knowledge bases across languages, which can then be incorporated into MT language models.

The paper notes that using Semantic Web technologies in MT is still “in its infancy” and that applying them for semantic disambiguation through pre-editing or post-editing is tedious, especially for common words rather than named entities and when repeated application is needed. It also highlights practical limitations such as poor run-time performance of reasoners and the fact that approaches relying on SWT are constrained by issues like lack of large bilingual datasets for some languages and knowledge bases being mostly in English, limiting support for other languages.

Structural divergence and linguistic properties/features are other obstacles not mentioned in the abstract. The paper also lists five additional open challenges: excessive focus on English/European languages and poor research on low-resource pairs, limitations across domains, translating non-standard social-network text, difficult translation among morphologically rich languages, and mismatch between speech-translation training data and real user speech.

The EEG features used were five statistical features: root mean square, zero crossing rate, moving window average, kurtosis, and power spectral entropy. After reducing the 155 EEG features to 30 using KPCA, the authors also computed delta and delta-delta features, giving a final EEG feature dimension of 90.

The baselines were speech recognition using only EEG features and speech recognition using concatenated EEG + MFCC acoustic features; for larger-vocabulary CTC experiments, MFCC-only results were also reported as a baseline. The paper also compared against using EEG features from only the T7 and T8 electrodes versus using all 31 EEG sensors with dimension reduction.
The subjects were asked to speak the first 30 sentences from the USC-TIMIT database. The paper also built two simultaneous speech-EEG recording datasets, called data set A and data set B.

Yes. On the QQP dataset at 10× compression, LadaBERT-4 achieves 67.4/86.6 while Distilled-BiLSTM achieves 68.2/88.1, so not there; however, on MNLI-m and MNLI-mm, LadaBERT-4 scores 75.8 and 76.1 versus Distilled-BiLSTM’s 73.0 and 72.6, respectively.

Yes. In Section 4.3 and 4.5 they compare weight pruning, matrix factorization, and hybrid pruning, and conclude that combining matrix factorization and weight pruning is better than either single method, while LadaBERT’s combination with knowledge distillation shows the strongest performance.
MNLI-m, MNLI-mm, SST-2, QQP, and QNLI.
News articles, specifically DailyMail news articles and the DUC 2002 news corpus.
They use two datasets created from DailyMail news articles and corresponding highlights for training, and evaluate on the DUC 2002 single-document summarization corpus and on a DailyMail test set.

Yes. They include a neural abstractive summarization baseline (NN-ABS) with an open vocabulary during decoding, described as a hierarchical document-level extension of Rush et al. (2015), and compare it against their extractive models in the experiments.
They discuss hierarchical commonsense knowledge, especially transitive entailment and ontological IS-A relations. They also mention non-hierarchical commonsense knowledge from raw text.

They mean embedding spaces whose geometric structure directly encodes the ontology’s partial-order constraints, so predictions automatically satisfy consistency properties such as transitivity. In the order-embedding model, this geometry is coordinate-wise: a supercategory embedding is smaller than a subcategory embedding in every coordinate.

Yes. They used pre-trained sentiment models, including a bilateral LSTM trained on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank and targeted sentiment classifiers pre-trained on 82 million Amazon reviews, which were applied without further adaptation.

The models extracted word unigrams and bigrams transformed with TF-IDF, 300-dimensional word2vec-based segment representations obtained by averaging word embeddings, and sentiment features from Empath’s 192 lexical categories plus pre-trained sentiment embeddings learned with a biLSTM on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank. They also experimented with GloVe and Sent2Vec.
2 languages.
Yes. The paper states that their systems outperformed the other competitors for both languages, and the best official Spanish polarity score was 0.39, higher than the other teams’ 0.04 and 0.05.

The multimodal resources consist of diagrams that describe topics in elementary school natural sciences. Both resources contain the same diagrams and represent their structure using graphs, but differ in their annotation schema and whether the annotations were created by crowd-sourced workers or trained experts.
No. The document states that AI2D was annotated by crowd-sourced non-expert annotators on Amazon Mechanical Turk, while AI2D-RST was annotated by trained experts in multimodal communication.

They are compared through two experiments using graph neural networks on the same diagrams: node classification and graph classification. AI2D provides the crowd-sourced graphs, while AI2D-RST provides expert-annotated grouping and grouping+connectivity graphs, and their performance is compared using accuracy, macro F1, and weighted F1 over 20 runs, with statistical significance tested using the Mann–Whitney U test.
Amazon Mechanical Turk.

The document contrasts crowd-sourced workers with trained experts, and states that AI2D-RST diagrams “have been annotated by trained experts” using a new multi-layer annotation schema. It also refers to “expert annotators” as those trained experts.
They opt for neural architectures using both a Hierarchical Attention Network (HAN), based on bi-directional GRUs, and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN).

They use the Clueweb09-derived dataset of [4] for baseline comparison, and for the main cross-temporal, cross-topic, and cross-domain experiments they generate a new Wikipedia crawl-based dataset based on Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” from 2018 and 2009.

Cross-domain. It has the lowest best F1 score among the setups discussed: cross-temporal 0.696, cross-topic 0.753, and cross-domain 0.645; the across-annotators section provides correlations rather than directly comparable setup difficulty.

They use weak-signal data from a Wikipedia crawl-based dataset: controversial seed pages from Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” (2018 and 2009), expanded by crawling “See also,” “References,” and “External links” up to two hops, with non-controversial seeds from Wikipedia’s random article endpoint. Labels are weakly propagated so that pages linked from controversial issues are assumed controversial.
Yes. They compare the semantic neural approaches (CNN and HAN) against lexical baselines such as TfIdf-SVM and language models (LM, LM-DBPedia, LM-wiki).
About 64 million segments from YouTube videos, comprising 1.2 billion tokens.
About 64 million segments from YouTube videos, comprising a total of 1.2 billion tokens.

The videos were represented by 1500-dimensional frame-level features extracted from video frames at 1-second intervals, similar to features used for large-scale image classification tasks. For a K-second video and N > K wordpieces, each feature was uniformly allocated to N/K wordpieces.
Yes. The main NMT architecture used in the experiments implements the attention mechanism, specifically the “scaled Luong” version, and the update section also discusses a Transformer model.

The paper explores an established NMT architecture based on LSTMs with attention, and it also experimented with the Transformer model. In the update on Facebook Research integration datasets, it further compares the small NMT model, a default OpenNMT model, and the Transformer model.

Symbolic rewriting is rewriting between symbolic expressions, such as terms, by applying rewrite rules; in the paper this includes pairs of terms rewritten from one to another with an equation and polynomial terms transformed from an arbitrary form to a normalized form. It is studied as a formal-to-formal translation task.

They incorporate expert knowledge by having experts specify anchor variables/terms—e.g., disease-specific words like “diabetes,” “insulin,” or “sleep apnea”—and adding a mutual-information term between those anchor variables and latent factors in the objective. This Anchored CorEx objective preserves information about the anchors while still explaining correlations in the data, letting anchors seed topics and impose semantics on the learned latent factors.
They evaluate on two corpora: 20 Newsgroups and the i2b2 2008 Obesity Challenge dataset.
No. They discuss seeded latent Dirichlet allocation (SeededLDA) only in related work, but their reported comparisons are against Unsupervised CorEx and a Naive Bayes baseline.

Peng and Dredze (2016) is the state-of-the-art NER system in Chinese social media. The paper’s integrated training model, F-Score Driven Model II, achieves a new state-of-the-art NER system in Chinese social media.
Sina Weibo service in China.

They used a modified labelled Weibo NER corpus as in Peng and Dredze (2016) for Chinese social media, and the same unlabelled text from Sina Weibo service in China. The unlabeled data consisted of 112,971,734 Weibo messages.
The five downstream tasks are XNLI (cross-lingual NLI), MLDoc (document classification), cross-lingual intent classification, POS tagging, and named entity recognition.

Yes. The paper repeatedly states that gains are more pronounced for low-resource languages, noting improved translation quality on low-resource languages and larger cross-lingual transfer gains on low-resource languages in tasks like XNLI and POS tagging.
No. The paper says mBERT is pre-trained on monolingual data in 104 languages, and in one experiment they train one model jointly on all languages “instead of training one model for each language.”

They were selected by taking the intersection of the languages that have POS labels in the Universal Dependencies dataset and the languages supported by the mNMT model. For POS tagging, this yielded 48 languages.

They use both automatic and human evaluations. Automatic evaluation uses perplexity and BLEU against human-annotated responses, while human evaluation uses ACUTE-EVAL self-chat A/B comparisons where annotators choose the better full dialogue in terms of engagingness, interestingness, and humanness.

The paper states there is “a significant gap” between the cross-lingual model and the other models, and that in human evaluation M-CausalBert against XNLG achieved “nearly 100 percent winning rate.” It does not provide a single numeric lag amount across models beyond this.

They compare against translation-pipeline models that translate the target-language query into English, use an English Poly-encoder model, and then translate the English response back into the target language: target query → English query → English response → target response. They denote this translation-based baseline as Poly.
Chinese, French, Indonesian, Italian, Korean, and Japanese.
Yes. They present their own large-scale contrastive pronoun test set, extracted automatically from the OpenSubtitles corpus.

The baselines are a standard bidirectional RNN model with attention that operates on the sentence level and does not use additional context, and a standard context-agnostic Transformer. The paper also considers simple concatenation baselines: concat22 for both RNN and Transformer, and concat21 for the Transformer.

The experimented context-aware models were recurrent concat22, s-hier, s-t-hier, and s-hier-to-2, including tied variants of the multi-encoder models; and Transformer-based concat21, concat22, and the context-aware Transformer of Voita et al. (2018). They also mention a sentence-level baseline, but it is context-agnostic.
They experimented on English→German. The paper focuses on translating the English pronoun “it” into the German pronouns “er,” “sie,” and “es.”

They obtain the entity linking results by using a simple EL algorithm that directly links a mention to the entity with the greatest commonness score, where commonness is computed from Wikipedia anchor links as the probability of an entity given the mention string. They use this commonness score as the confidence of the linking result, and within the same document they also apply a heuristic to corefer generic person mentions to more specific ones.

They use a deep neural fine-grained entity typing model with two layers of BiLSTMs for context encoding and a three-layer MLP for prediction. The model combines context representation, mention string representation, KB type representation from entity linking, and the entity linking confidence score.
They evaluate on FIGER (GOLD) and BBN. OntoNotes is not used.
1 domain expert.
The evaluation metrics used are precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy. The paper also reports accuracy without the ‘O’-tag.
The fine-tuned BERT model achieved an F1-score of 0.89 on the concept recognition dataset. Its overall accuracy was 0.97, and accuracy without the 'O' tag was 0.86.
Yes. The paper states that implementation with demo and dataset is available at https://github.com/jitinkrishnan/NASA-SE.

The paper uses a BIO labelling scheme, where B marks the beginning, I marks the inside/continuation, and O marks outside of an entity. The concept labels are abb, grp, syscon, opcon, seterm, event, org, art, cardinal, loc, and mea.
BERT’s pre-trained cased language model.
The constructed CR dataset contains roughly 3700 sentences annotated at the word-token level.
Precision, recall and F-measure were computed to evaluate the classification performance.

The hand-crafted features mentioned are Teufel et al.’s sixteen types of features, such as sentence position, sentence length, and tense, as well as Widyantoro et al.’s content features, qualifying adjectives, and meta-discourse features. The paper also refers to a cuewords matching method.

The paper uses word2vec word embeddings, specifically learned with the Skip-gram/CBOW word2vec framework, and also trains BAS-specific word embeddings (BSWE). It additionally compares paragraph vectors (PV-DM) as an extension of word2vec.
No. They use the existing Argumentative Zoning Corpus, which consists of 80 AZ-annotated conference articles.

Sentence embeddings were generated in three ways: by averaging the word vectors in a sentence, by learning paragraph vectors with the PV-DM paragraph2vec model, and by training BAS-specific word embeddings and then averaging those word vectors in the sentence. In the averaging model, the sentence vector is the mean of the learned word2vec embeddings of its words.

Argumentative zoning (AZ), also called rhetorical sentence classification, is the process of assigning rhetorical status to extracted sentences. It provides readers with general discourse context so scientific ideas can be better linked, compared, and analyzed.

They crawled two credit-based freemium blackmarket sites, YouLikeHits and Like4Like, from February to April 2019, created dummy accounts on these sites, and recorded the Tweet IDs of tweets posted there to gain retweets. They then used Twitter’s REST API to collect the tweet objects, and also collected the authors’ timelines to obtain genuine tweets by the same users that had not been posted to those blackmarket sites.

They compare against state-of-the-art Twitter spam detection methods and feature-concatenation baselines. Specifically, the baselines are Spam Detection 1 (Wu et al. [5]), Spam Detection 2 (Rajdev et al. [12]), and classifiers trained on concatenated features: Random Forest, MLP, and SVM.
English.
They look at freemium blackmarket services, specifically credit-based freemium services. The two blackmarket sites they crawled are YouLikeHits and Like4Like.
They use English, Chinese, and French during pretraining. The paper also initializes from a 15-language pre-trained XLM model.

It is a Transformer-based decoder in a sequence-to-sequence model. In training details, the model uses a 6-layer decoder, and each Transformer layer has 1024 hidden units, 8 attention heads, and GELU activations.
The encoder is based on a Transformer, and in the reported pre-trained XNLG setup it has 10 layers. Each Transformer layer uses 1024 hidden units, 8 attention heads, and GELU activations.

The baselines are XLM and machine-translation-based pipeline methods, specifically PIPELINE (XLM) and, in some experiments, PIPELINE (XLM) with Google Translator. For supervised English-English question generation, they also compare against CorefNQG and MP-GSN.

They use two human evaluation metrics: a triple pairing task where judges match shuffled stories to their corresponding prompts, and a blind human preference test where judges choose which story they prefer reading. The preference test is used to assess the importance of hierarchical generation.
Perplexity and prompt ranking accuracy.

They compare against four baseline types: (1) non-hierarchical language models that do not condition on the prompt, including the gated convolutional language model (GCNN) and GCNN with self-attention; (2) seq2seq models using LSTMs, convolutional seq2seq, and Conv seq2seq with decoder self-attention; (3) an ensemble of two Conv seq2seq with self-attention models; and (4) a KNN retrieval model that finds the closest training prompt using TF-IDF vectors created with FASTTEXT and FAISS.
No. They use convolutional seq2seq and GCNN language models with self-attention, and their fusion method uses a pretrained seq2seq model, not BERT.
The premise is generated using the convolutional language model from Dauphin et al. (2017). For prompt generation, they use a self-attentive GCNN language model.

Yes. The paper describes the dataset as consisting of human-written stories from Reddit’s WritingPrompts forum for “creative text generation,” where users submit story premises and others respond with stories.
The stories are collected from Reddit’s WritingPrompts forum. They scraped three years of prompts and their associated stories using the official Reddit API.
word2vec, fastText, GloVe, Baroni, and SL999.

The datasets used were SICK, STSB, and MRPC. Specifically, SICK was used for textual entailment recognition and also for semantic textual similarity, STSB for semantic textual similarity, and Microsoft Research Paraphrase Corpus (MRPC) for paraphrase identification.

They compare with DT TEAM (Maharjan et al., 2017), ECNU (Tian et al., 2017), BIT (Wu et al., 2017), TF-KLD (Ji and Eisenstein, 2013), Multi-Perspective CNN (He et al., 2015), InferSent (Conneau et al., 2017), GRAN (Wieting and Gimpel, 2017), Paragram-Phrase (Wieting et al., 2016b), and HCTI (Shao, 2017).
Fleiss’s Kappa is used for inter-annotator agreement.
Yes. They report inter-annotator agreement using Fleiss’s Kappa in Table 3 for their labeled Twitter data.
170 workers participated.

The social-network features are neighborhood overlap measures between author and target in the directed following network: downward overlap, upward overlap, inward overlap, outward overlap, and bidirectional overlap. The paper also uses user-based profile metrics from both accounts: friend count, follower count, verified status, and number of tweets posted within six-month timeline snapshots.
The five factors considered are aggression, repetition, harmful intent, visibility among peers, and power imbalance.

Cyberbullying is not given a single standardized definition in the paper; instead, the authors found that available definitions contain a strict subset of five criteria: aggression, repetition, harmful intent, visibility among peers, and power imbalance. The paper also notes a widely cited traditional bullying definition based on repetition, harmful intent, and an imbalance of power.

The output was evaluated objectively for similarity to training data using Phrase Overlap match and K-gram-Jaccard similarity, subjectively for humor/content quality using human judgment, and for syntactic correctness using the Link Grammar Parser. They also measured overall syntactic accuracy as the percentage of sentences having at least one valid linkage at Null Count 0.
The joke data came from two sources: CrowdTruth and Subreddits. After cleaning, they ended up with 96,910 jokes.
Inspirational quotes.
They use filters of size 1 × 3 in the convolution layer.

On FB15k-237, they report outperforming the previous state-of-the-art by 0.523 − 0.418 = 0.105 in MRR and 59.3% − 53.2% = 6.1% absolute in Hits@10. On WN18RR, the paper states CapsE obtains the best mean rank, but does not quantify by how much over the previous state of the art.
No. Their experiments were based on BERT, although the paper mentions other pre-trained language models such as Open-GPT in the introduction and related work.

They compared BERT with BERT + Bi-LSTM, BERT + HighwayLSTM, BERT + DenseNet, BERT + BIMPM, BERT + BIMPM (first bi-LSTM removed), and BERT + Sim-Transformer. They also report an ensembled model in the text classification experiment.
The testing datasets were CoNLL03 for named entity recognition, Yahoo Answer Classification Dataset for text classification, and Quora-Question-Pair for semantic similarity tasks.

They obtained high inter-annotator agreement: raw agreement for scene/function/construal was .92/.95/.90 in Phase 1 and .93/.90/.89 in Phase 2. Cohen’s kappa was .90/.93/.88 in Phase 1 and .92/.88/.88 in Phase 2.

They manually annotated a Chinese translation of The Little Prince using the SNACS framework: after Jieba tokenization, they manually corrected segmentation, jointly identified adposition targets according to their Mandarin criteria, and labeled each target with supersenses including scene role, function, and construal. The corpus was annotated by three native Mandarin Chinese speakers; 25% of adposition targets were labeled cooperatively by all three, and the rest were labeled independently and then cooperatively adjudicated, with annotations stored in CoNLL-U-Lex format.
The final corpus includes all 27 chapters of The Little Prince, with a total of 20k tokens. More specifically, Table 2 reports 20,287 tokens, 1,597 sentences, and 933 adpositions.

They use a fake news dataset whose training set has five features: ID, title, author, text, and label, with about 20,800 samples. They do not use the provided test dataset because it has no labels, and instead randomly select test data from the training set for evaluation.

The paper explores three pre-training text representation models: CountVectorizer, TF-IDFVectorizer, and Word2Vec, and five fine-tuning classification models: Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Long Short-Term Memory networks (LSTMs), Random Forest, Logistic Regression, and Support Vector Machine (SVM). It also mentions possible future models such as GloVe, Transformer, and BERT.

Six groups of features are extracted: numerical features, language-model features, word-cluster features, topic-model features, part-of-speech tag features, and essay-text bag-of-words features. The numerical features include essay statistics and readability/complexity measures such as sentence/word/letter counts, Gunning Fog index, difficult words, misspellings, duplicate words, and low-idf words. The essay text is represented with idf-weighted bigram bag-of-words, POS tags with unigram bag-of-words, topic models with LDA document-topic distributions, word clusters with one-hot cluster vectors, and language models with essay probabilities under two trigram models.

The evaluation metrics were the official challenge measure \(E\), a cost-based misclassification error computed from the confusion matrix and a prior cost matrix, and accuracy. Results were reported using stratified 3-fold cross-validation.
Gradient Boosted Trees trained with gradient-based one-side sampling, implemented in Microsoft’s LightGBM toolkit.

Future work is to investigate whether the usefulness of LDA topic distributions comes from their modeling power or from a dataset artifact caused by essay subjects depending on learner level, which could create information leak. The paper suggests that further analysis and validation could answer this if essay topics are released so validation splits can be done based on topics.
For reference, the baseline was a tuned Logistic Regression with the same class weights for the class imbalance. It achieved error E = 10.10 and accuracy 97.2.

Sentence alignment quality is evaluated by manual human evaluation after splitting the corpus into three bins by hunalign confidence score: (−0.3, 0.3], (0.3, 0.8], and (0.8, ∞). Annotators rated text-to-text alignments on a 5-point scale from 1 “wrong alignment” to 5 “correct alignment and fully compositional translation,” with two annotators each rating 30 items per bin and agreement measured by Krippendorff’s α.

Speech alignment quality is evaluated by manual human annotation of audio-text alignments on a 3-point scale: 1 = wrong alignment, 2 = partial alignment with some words or sentences missing, and 3 = correct alignment, allowing non-spoken syllables at the start or end. Two annotators each rated 30 items from each of three bins, with 10 shared items for inter-annotator reliability; the agreement for audio-text alignment was measured by Krippendorff’s α and was 1.00.

Yes. The paper explicitly states that their pooling-equipped gating mechanism is designed to overcome the problem caused by one-sentence bags, and claims it performs stably even when only one sentence appears in a bag by assigning low gating values to wrongly labeled instances.

Yes. They show one-sentence-bag examples in Table 1 and Table 6, and they also report experiments on only one-sentence bags where SeG outperforms selective attention: on extracted one-sentence bags, SeG achieves AUC 0.48 and 90% accuracy versus PCNN+ATT’s AUC 0.35 and 78% accuracy.

They outperform the previous state-of-the-art approaches in top-N precision by 10.3% over PCNN+HATT and 5.3% over PCNN+BAG-ATT. They also improve over PCNN+ATT+SL by 7.8% in mean P@N for all sentences.

For semantic relatedness, their CharCNN+MB-LSTM+Temp-CNN achieves Pearson 0.851, outperforming existing reported methods by up to 0.052 over MaxEnt (0.799) and by 0.023 over the best listed traditional ensemble (0.828), though it is 0.012 below the 0.863 tree-LSTM result of Tai et al. For textual entailment, it reaches 0.842 accuracy, improving over SVM (0.823), Decision Tree (0.831), and the ensemble (0.836) by 0.019, 0.011, and 0.006 respectively, but it is 0.004 below the best listed MaxEnt result (0.846).
They evaluate on the standard SICK (Sentences Involving Compositional Knowledge) dataset for two tasks: semantic relatedness and textual entailment.

No. They report task performance for end-to-end models such as WordEmbedding+MB-LSTM+Temp-CNN, CharCNN+MB-LSTM+Temp-CNN, and LSTM variants with/without CNN, but they do not separately evaluate the learned representations before forwarding them to the CNN layer.
Unanswerable
The challenge used CTS (Conversational Telephone Speech) data. The paper refers to it as the NIST 2019 speaker recognition evaluation CTS challenge.
The fused system outperformed all individual subsystems. Among the six subsystems, eftdnn performed best overall.

Yes. The paper’s main idea is that frequencies of semantically related words and phrases that co-occur in the same texts should be enhanced, and this is intended to increase their contribution to topics.
They explore economic and political domains with EuroVoc, the banking domain with the Russian banking collection and RuThes, and the Islam domain in the manual evaluation using an Islam thesaurus.

They experimented with three thesauri: WordNet, the European Union information-retrieval thesaurus EuroVoc, and the Russian thesaurus RuThes. For the Islam site analysis, they used RuThes together with an additional Islam thesaurus.

They define hate speech as language used to express hatred toward a targeted group or intended to be derogatory, humiliating, or insulting toward members of that group. In extreme cases, it may also include language that threatens or incites violence.
They train a multi-class classifier to distinguish hate speech, offensive language, and neither. Their final model is a one-versus-rest logistic regression with L2 regularization.
33,458 Twitter users.

They extracted the timeline for each of 33,458 users, resulting in a corpus of 85.4 million tweets. From this corpus, they randomly sampled 25k tweets and obtained 24,802 labeled tweets after removing cases without a majority label.
SST-2. More specifically, the large variants of BERT and RoBERTa on SST-2 show improved quality when 12–16 layers are frozen instead of fine-tuning all layers.
Yes. They evaluate both RoBERTa BASE and RoBERTa LARGE, including reproduced results and large-model experiments.

On cross-dataset results, their method improves over the baseline by up to 7.63 accuracy points on JOCI when trained on MultiNLI (48.81% vs. 41.18%) and by up to 1.38 points on SICK when trained on SNLI (53.45% vs. 52.07%) or 1.61 points when trained on MultiNLI (58.35% vs. 56.74%). The paper does not compare directly against external state-of-the-art models, only against its own baseline.

The paper says they propose “an easy-adopting debiasing training framework,” and cites Zhang et al. (2019), which investigated selection bias in six natural language sentence matching datasets and proposed a debiasing training and evaluation framework. However, it does not explicitly state that their proposed framework could be applied to other NLP problems.

It mitigates the bias by balancing artifacts across labels through sample reweighting: each training example is weighted by the inverse of the estimated hypothesis-only label probability, \(w \approx 1/P(y|h)\), with smoothing \(w = 1/((1-\epsilon)P(y|h)+\epsilon)\). The probabilities \(P(y|h)\) are estimated via K-fold cross-prediction, and label-wise weight sums are normalized to keep priors unchanged, making artifacts less predictive during training.
They use SNLI, MultiNLI, JOCI, and SICK. Specifically, they train and validate on SNLI and MultiNLI, and test on SICK and JOCI for cross-dataset evaluation.

The entropy algorithm was the most helpful strategy for building machine learning models fast and efficiently. Vote entropy and KL divergence were also helpful for training machine learning ensemble classifiers.
3,685,984 unique tweets.
The IDN Tagged Corpus consists of 10K sentences and 250K tokens.

The neural network models explored were feedforward networks and bidirectional LSTMs (biLSTMs), with prediction layers using either softmax or CRF. They also considered a previously cited feedforward neural network model for Indonesian POS tagging.

The rule-based model evaluated was the Indonesian rule-based POS tagger by Rashel et al. [15]. It tags named entities and multi-word expressions using a dictionary, uses MorphInd to tag the remaining words, and applies 15 hand-crafted rules to resolve ambiguous tags.
The datasets mentioned for Indonesian POS tagging are the PANL10N dataset and the IDN Tagged Corpus. In this paper, the models were evaluated on the IDN Tagged Corpus.
They use parts of Chinese Wikipedia articles, resulting in 11,529,432 segmented words for training.
No. They evaluate only on two tasks: word similarity and word analogy.
Unanswerable

The paper identifies that the model lacked flexibility for out-of-vocabulary words, with performance dropping substantially on the OOV test set. It also states that for unknown words the model assigned a zero vector, which negatively affected the testing results.

Yes. The POS tagger experiments evaluate “POS and morph classes” using precision, recall, and F1 score, and the paper states it investigates taggers that classify morphological features versus only part of speech.

It extends the standard ENAMEX types of organization, location, and person by adding a fourth class, facility (FAC). The resulting keyword list for training consisted of 4 classes: LOC, ORG, PERSON, and FAC.
Gender, number, and case.
Yes. The reported experiments use a dataset of Twitter users from a pregnancy-related corpus, and the paper does not report results on any non-English datasets.

The dataset consisted of a sample of 10,417 Twitter users drawn from a database of more than 400 million publicly available tweets posted by more than 100,000 users who had announced their pregnancy on Twitter. Two professional annotators labeled users as “bot,” “non-bot,” or “unavailable” based on their publicly available Twitter sites, with “bot” defined broadly as users not appearing to post personal information and “unavailable” referring to accounts that could not be viewed; inter-annotator agreement on 1000 overlapping annotations was κ = 0.93. After resolving disagreements, 413 users (4%) were labeled bot, 7,849 (75.35%) non-bot, and 2,069 (19.9%) unavailable.

By using a machine learning classifier on top of the existing Botometer system and extending it with health-specific derived features. The added features included tweet diversity, URL score, mean and standard deviation of daily posts, topic heterogeneity from LDA over recent tweets, mean word length and standard deviation, number of faces detected in the profile picture, and presence or absence of a person’s name in the user name.

Adverse drug reaction detection, syndromic surveillance, subject recruitment for cancer trials, characterizing drug abuse, and longitudinal user-level analyses such as mining health-related information from cohorts of pregnant women.
Yes. The QA tuples come from a customer support dataset sourced from Twitter, so they fall under the customer support domain.
The baseline model is the pre-trained version of BERT without special emoji tokens, specifically bert-base-multilingual-cased.
The corpus contains 2000 question-answer tuples.

They used a customer support dataset from Twitter collected by Hori and Hori [12], containing 2000 question-answer tuples. It consists of conversations with at least one question and one free-form answer, with 170 of the 2000 tuples containing at least one emoji character.

They used boosting via a Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LGBM), described as fast gradient boosting on decision trees. The LGBM combined base classifier predictions with TF-IDF and QIEF features, including a setup combining both BERT and BioBERT base learners with those features.
Yes. They trained and compared two pretrained contextualized embeddings: the original BERT and BioBERT.
96,328 sequences.

The new dataset, PICONET, was created by collecting structured abstracts from PubMed and carefully selecting abstract headings representative of Population, Intervention, and Outcome categories. The authors searched PubMed on April 11, 2019 for 363,078 structured abstracts filtered for Clinical Trial, Humans, and English, lemmatized section labels to cluster similar headings, manually checked small samples to verify representativeness, kept full abstract sections rather than splitting them into sentences, added multi-label sections and negative examples, and cleaned the data with methods such as language identification, removing missing values, cleaning unicode characters, and filtering sequences between 5 and 200 words.
Unanswerable

The paper mentions that previous datasets/methods suffered from ambiguity and redundancy. In particular, keyword-based labeling could select sections that were not purely about one PIO category, and the dataset of Jin and Szolovits (2018) split multi-sentence labeled sections into individual sentences, which the authors argue can produce poor candidates and lose feature-rich context.
Unanswerable

They explore four types of structure-dependent agreement relations: subject-verb agreement, noun-determiner agreement, noun-attributive adjective agreement, and subject-predicate adjective agreement. They also study four morphosyntactic features involved in these relations: number, gender, case, and person.

They evaluate on seven text classification datasets: Amazon, Yelp, IMDB, MR, MPQA, Subj, and TREC. Amazon, Yelp, IMDB, and MR are sentiment classification datasets; MPQA is for opinion polarity detection; Subj is for subjective/objective classification; and TREC is for question type classification.
Their approach is compared to TextFooler as the baseline attack, and it is evaluated on three text classification models: word-LSTM, word-CNN, and a fine-tuned BERT base-uncased classifier.
It reduced error by 14% relative to prior work on the Amazon dataset. Using the same classifier settings as the prior best, it improved F1 from 0.744 to 0.752, a gain of 0.008.

The previous best-results model was Buschmeier, Cimiano, and Klinger (2014)’s logistic regression classifier using all tested feature types, including metadata, sentiment, grammar, interjections/laughter, emoticons, and bag-of-words features. It achieved an F1 of 0.744 on the Amazon dataset.

The baselines are All Sarcasm, which assumes every instance is sarcastic, and Random, which randomly assigns each instance as sarcastic or non-sarcastic. Their F1 scores are 0.515 and 0.345, respectively.

The paper explores two domains: Twitter and Amazon product reviews. It also mentions prior research in satirical news articles and the Internet Argument Corpus, and proposes future work in literature.

Training data came from two domains: Twitter and Amazon product reviews. The Amazon data was the publicly available sarcasm corpus of Filatova (2012), and the Twitter data consisted of tweets collected in February–March 2016 containing exactly one trailing hashtag among “#sarcasm,” “#happiness,” “#sadness,” “#anger,” “#surprise,” “#fear,” and “#disgust,” with #sarcasm tweets labeled sarcastic and the others labeled non-sarcastic.
The best model was LSTM and GRU with Attention, achieving a macro-F1 of 0.89. In Table 7, it also had weighted-average precision, recall, and F1 of 0.93, 0.88, and 0.88, respectively.

The models tested on the dataset were Linear SVM, RBF SVM, Stochastic Gradient Descent classifier, Multinomial Naïve Bayes, Bernoulli Naïve Bayes, Pooled GRU, Stacked LSTM with Attention, LSTM and GRU with Attention, 2D Convolution with Pooling, GRU with Capsule, LSTM with Capsule and Attention, and BERT-Base Multilingual Cased.
LSTM and GRU with Attention best performs on the offensive language identification task, achieving the highest macro-F1 score of 0.89.
No. The tweets were manually annotated by a team of volunteers using the LightTag annotation platform.
No. OGTD v1.0 is imbalanced, containing over 29% offensive content (1,401 offensive vs. 3,378 not offensive).

They experiment with five classical machine learning models: Linear SVM, RBF SVM, Stochastic Gradient Descent classifier, Multinomial Naïve Bayes, and Bernoulli Naïve Bayes. They also experiment with seven deep learning models: Pooled GRU, Stacked LSTM with Attention, LSTM and GRU with Attention, 2D Convolution with Pooling, GRU with Capsule, LSTM with Capsule and Attention, and BERT-Base Multilingual Cased.
Unanswerable
The published dataset contains 567 Catalan reviews and 343 Basque reviews, for a total of 910 reviews.
Three annotators. Two annotated the reviews, and a third annotator resolved conflicts after the final iteration.

The sentence construction component relies on the GF Library: after the system identifies a sentence’s structure and components, the GF grammar encoder assigns GF rules to that structure and builds constructors for the components, including complement components, while the exporter merges sentence-level grammars into a paragraph-level GF program. The GF Library then concatenates the provided strings according to the target language grammar to generate complete sentences.

The two use cases are: generating natural language descriptions for annotated ontologies, and creating an intermediate representation for Wikipedia pages that can be used for translation. These are presented in Sections 4.1 and 4.2, respectively.

Yes. They explore the structure of the language space, showing that the learned language vectors capture similarity and genetic relationships between languages, and they also study mixing and interpolating between languages and language variants.

A standard stacked character-based LSTM with two layers, followed by a hidden layer and a final softmax output layer. Language embedding vectors are concatenated to the inputs of the LSTMs at each time step and to the hidden layer before the softmax.

They show genetic relationships by hierarchically agglomerative clustering the learned language vectors. For example, Figure 5 presents a hierarchical clustering of Germanic language vectors that closely matches the established genetic relationships in that family, showing that language family trees can be reconstructed by clustering the vectors.

No. They tested MagiCoder by comparing its output with manually revised reports from VigiSegn, and only report preliminary qualitative results from daily activities that it reduces work, not a study on real pharmacologists measuring encoding time.

Yes. In the discussion, they hypothesize that connectives have minor relevance because ADR reports are often badly or hurriedly written and usually lack complex structure, which helps explain why the system works better on short descriptions than on longer ones.

The MagiCoder algorithm has five phases: (1) definition of ad hoc data structures, mainly hash tables for efficient access to MedDRA terms and their words; (2) preprocessing of the original ADR text, including tokenization, stemming, and removal of irrelevant words; (3) a word-by-word linear scan with a voting task, where each word votes for the LLTs it belongs to and matching information is stored.  
Then it performs (4) weight calculation for recognized terms using criteria such as coverage, type of coverage, coverage distance, and coverage density; and (5) selection, ordering, and release of winning terms, where voted terms are pruned, sorted, and the final best candidate terms are returned.

The system uses ad hoc hash-table data structures: it builds a meta-dictionary from MedDRA words (and another from their stems) so each input token can be checked in constant time against the set of LLTs containing it, then processes the narrative with a single word-by-word linear scan where words “vote” for matching terms. Because the meta-dictionaries are built once in \(O(km)\) time from the terminology and the narrative is scanned once using constant-time dictionary access, the method avoids expensive permutation/combination matching and is linear with respect to the size of the narrative input and the terminology.

They conclude that a more complete picture of the aspects and mechanisms of personal recovery in bipolar disorder requires complementing prior interview- and questionnaire-based evidence with large-scale analysis of first-person online accounts. The authors expect this to reveal not only known recovery aspects, such as those in CHIME, but also potentially novel aspects and factors that facilitate or hinder recovery, especially across more heterogeneous, multilingual, and culturally diverse populations.
Unanswerable

The analysis was to use a mixed-methods, explanatory sequential design: exploratory statistical analysis of large social-media corpora followed by manual qualitative investigation of selected examples. Computationally, it planned comparative corpus analysis with Wmatrix for keywords and key concepts, iterative dictionary building plus semantic tagging with USAS, distributional semantics for co-occurring terms, unsupervised topic modelling via DLATK, and emotion/sentiment analysis using supervised machine learning, including multilingual embeddings for cross-lingual transfer.

No. For Twitter and Reddit data, the document states that implicit consent is assumed for public posts and that the researchers will not ask account holders for consent; only blog authors will be contacted to obtain informed consent.

Twitter and Reddit users with a BD diagnosis will be identified automatically via self-reported diagnosis statements, such as “I was diagnosed with BD-I last week,” by extending diagnosis patterns and terms from Cohan et al. (2018). Relevant blogs will be manually identified, and their authors will be contacted to obtain informed consent for using their texts.

The training/testing data comes from CCPC1.0, a Chinese Classical Poetry Corpus consisting of 834,902 poems in total. For CI experiments, the paper also mentions selecting 6 Cipais with relatively sufficient training samples in CPCC.

The generated Chinese poetry types are SHI and CI. For SHI, the model generates Jueju and Lvshi, including Wuyan Jueju, Qiyan Jueju, Wuyan Lvshi, and Qiyan Lvshi; for CI, it generates 121 major forms (Cipai).

They compare mainly to two previous methods for reducing OOV rates: the subword method of Sennrich, Haddow, and Birch (2015b) and the fully character-based method of Lee, Cho, and Hofmann (2016). They also mention comparisons against large-vocabulary approaches such as Jean et al. (2014) and Mi, Wang, and Ittycheriah (2016).
Yes. They train both skip-gram (SG) and CBOW models using the word2vec tool.

The quality of the word vectors is measured by evaluation tasks: word similarity, synonym selection, and especially word analogy. In the word similarity task, quality is measured by the Pearson correlation coefficient between human similarity judgments and cosine distances between word vectors; in synonym selection and word analogy, it is measured by task accuracy.
No. They report results on multiple datasets, including KPTimes, JPTimes, KPCrowd, DUC-2001, SemEval, and KP20k.
The news texts were collected by crawling selected online news websites, primarily The New York Times, with an additional secondary source from The Japan Times.
The baselines used are FirstPhrases and the unsupervised MultipartiteRank.

State-of-the-art neural keyphrase generation models are trained, specifically CopyRNN-based encoder-decoder models with a copying mechanism. Two such models are trained: CopySci on scientific abstracts (KP20k) and CopyNews on newspaper articles (KPTimes).

Editors’ annotations are more uniform and consistent because editors revise algorithm-suggested tags and use a more controlled indexing vocabulary, whereas existing datasets mostly contain non-expert author or reader annotations that are less controlled, include seldom-used variants or misspellings, or are highly extractive. They also contain a much higher proportion of absent keyphrases—about 55% in KPTimes—showing that editors generalize more and assign keyphrases that do not appear in the document, unlike reader annotations where most keyphrases are present in the text.
The dataset is SensEval-3 lexical sample task data, consisting of 57 ambiguous words in total: 20 nouns, 32 verbs, and 5 adjectives. It has separate training and test samples.
Yes. They initialize the word embeddings with pre-trained GloVe vectors for the context words.
6 layers.
They use F-measure (%) as the evaluation metric for reporting WSD performance. They also mention accuracy in terms of F-measure.
They used four corpora: the DUC-2001 dataset, the Inspec dataset, the NUS Keyphrase Corpus, and the ICSI Meeting Corpus.
27.53 on DUC, 27.01 on Inspec, 4.30 on ICSI, and 9.10 on NUS.
The state-of-the-art models mentioned are SingleRank and Topical PageRank. The paper states that WikiRank improves over these models by more than 2% in F1-score.

They do not directly verify correctness; they use the ART teacher model’s encoder-decoder attention distributions as word-alignment hints, assuming good attentions usually lead to accurate translation while poor ones may cause wrong output tokens. They encourage the NART student to match these alignments by minimizing the KL-divergence between the teacher’s and student’s per-head encoder-decoder attention distributions.

The Transformer ART baseline has an inference latency of 784 ms per sentence. The paper also states their NART model is one order of magnitude faster, with 30.2× speedup without rescoring and 17.8× with teacher rescoring.
BLEU score.
Yes. Besides WMT14 En-De and De-En, the model was also evaluated on IWSLT14 German-to-English (De-En).
Unanswerable

The most discriminating features are average, maximum, minimum, and standard deviation, with average, maximum, and minimum having the highest information gain. Probability and proportionality features have low information gain.

On the alternate datasets (DSLCC development set), LDR, Skip-gram, and SenVec obtain comparable results, supporting the robustness of LDR. LDR scores are: Bulgarian 99.9, Macedonian 99.9, Spain Spanish 84.7, Argentina Spanish 88.0, Portugal Portuguese 87.4, Brazilian Portuguese 90.0, Bosnian 78.0, Croatian 85.8, Serbian 86.4, Indonesian 99.4, Malay 99.2, Czech 99.8, Slovak 99.3, and Other languages 99.9.

No. The paper says extracting answer spans by looking for occurrences of the gold standard answer in the provided snippets “is not perfect” and can produce false negatives because “a sentence answers the question, but the exact string used is not in the synonym list,” but it does not report the coverage of answers being substrings.
Unanswerable
A neural tensor network (NTN) is used to make the predictions. For intent embedding, the paper uses a BiLSTM, and for sentiment embedding, a softmax binary classifier.

The event prediction task is evaluated as script event prediction on the standard multiple choice narrative cloze (MCNC) dataset, following Li et al. (2018a). Using the SGNN framework with the proposed event embeddings replacing the input embeddings, performance is measured by Accuracy on the test set.

The paper uses the New York Times Gigaword Corpus (LDC2007T07) for pre-training event embeddings and ATOMIC for intent and sentiment annotations. For evaluation, it uses the hard similarity dataset from Weber et al. (2018) (230 event pairs) plus an extended 1,000-pair version annotated by the authors, the transitive sentence similarity dataset, and the standard multiple choice narrative cloze (MCNC) dataset.
No. They compare against SGNS, PPMI-SVD, C2V-NO-ATT, Porter Stemmer, and Morfessor, but fastText is not mentioned.

Yes. The paper says English is morphologically impoverished and that the model performs better on words with richer morphology, and it expects the model to perform even better for languages with richer morphology than English, such as Turkish and German.
They only report results for English. They expect the model to perform even better for morphologically richer languages such as Turkish and German.

They are evaluated by comparing model cosine similarities for word pairs to human similarity judgments and measuring the Spearman’s ρ correlation between the model rankings and human rankings. The comparison uses the WordSim353, MEN test, and Rare Word datasets.
The margin between their best-performing HABCNN-TE and NR is 15.6/16.5 (accuracy/NDCG) on MCTest-150 and 7.3/4.6 on MCTest-500.

They compared against manually engineered-feature MCTest systems including Narasimhan and Barzilay (2015), Sachan et al. (2015), Wang and McAllester (2015), and Smith et al. (2015), and deep learning approaches including memory networks (Weston et al., 2014; Weston et al., 2015; Kumar et al., 2015), Neural Reasoner (Peng et al., 2015), QANTA (Iyyer et al., 2014), an attention-based LSTM reader over news text (Hermann et al., 2015), and answer selection systems by Yu et al. (2014), Yang et al. (2015), Severyn and Moschitti (2015), Shen et al. (2015), and Wang et al. (2010). In experiments, the baselines were Addition, Addition-proj, Neural Reasoner (NR), and the Attentive Reader (AR).
The baseline is a one-stage RNN system. It uses the same last-stage RNN structure with a 2-layer BLSTM and is trained directly to recognize dialect category.

They achieve 88.88% accuracy overall with the two-stage BLSTM system, with 87.72% on utterances ≤3s and 90.04% on utterances >3s. The three-stage BLSTM system gets 87.24% overall, and the baseline gets 78.85%; the paper also states they achieve high accuracy of 89% and won first place in the Xunfei Chinese Dialect Recognition Challenge.
The 10 dialects explored are Ningxia, Hefei, Sichuan, Shanxi, Changsha, Hebei, Nanchang, Shanghai, Kekka, and Fujian.
Transformer architecture.
Unanswerable

The restricted track system does not use any external NLP tools, spell checkers, or hand-crafted confusion sets; it relies entirely on neural models. The organizers provided 1.2M pairs of corrected and uncorrected sentences for this track.
Unanswerable
There is no clear winner according to human evaluation. MODULAR reduces bias better, while CONCURRENT is more fluent and preserves meaning better.
They were a Wiki community effort: the corpus “consists of aligned sentences pre and post-neutralization by English Wikipedia editors” and was “harvested from Wikipedia edits.”

Subjective text is automatically neutralized by suggesting edits that preserve the original meaning while removing inappropriate subjectivity. The paper proposes two encoder-decoder methods: an interpretable MODULAR system that first uses a BERT-based classifier to detect problematic words and then edits them via a join embedding, and a simpler CONCURRENT system that uses a BERT encoder to identify subjectivity during generation.

The paper investigates American Sign Language (ASL) recognition, specifically word-level recognition of 51 ASL signs using multimodal data. It uses skeletal and RGB video data for sign language recognition.
The best model is Spatial AI-LSTM, with an average cross-subject accuracy of 81% (standard deviation 6%).
